title: When Motherhood Becomes Advocacy: Eden Marie James on Medical Freedom and Change
guestBio: Eden Marie James is a certified clinical nutritionist, mother, and passionate advocate who founded the MAHA Mom Coalition after her child experienced a vaccine injury. She has spent nearly a decade working globally to combat child trafficking and is a fierce voice for medical freedom and parental rights.
excerpt: After her child's vaccine injury, Eden Marie James didn't stay silent—she became a certified nutritionist, founded a grassroots coalition, and turned her pain into purpose. Her story will inspire you to lead where you are and fight for what matters.
metaDescription: Eden Marie James shares how her child's injury led to founding MAHA Mom Coalition, fighting for medical freedom, and becoming an advocate for change.
keyTakeaways: Personal pain can become your most powerful platform for creating change in the world.
Motherhood often reveals leadership capabilities you never knew you had.
Medical freedom and parental rights require active advocacy from informed mothers.
Grassroots movements led by passionate mothers can drive significant policy change.
Storytelling is one of the most essential tools in advocacy and leadership.
Global issues like child trafficking require local leaders willing to take action.
One woman's courage to speak up can inspire an entire movement of change.

pullQuotes.text: Eden's response wasn't silence — it was action.
This conversation is about the invisible leadership of mothers, the courage to speak up when no one else will, and the power of turning personal pain into public purpose.

timestamps.label: Eden's Story of Injury and Transformation

content:
Some of the most powerful leaders in the world never set out to lead. They were simply mothers who refused to stay silent when their children needed them most. This is exactly the story of Eden Marie James, whose journey from devastated parent to fierce advocate demonstrates the extraordinary leadership that emerges when motherhood meets injustice.

In this compelling episode of The Manage Her, host Aimee Rickabus sits down with Eden to explore a conversation that goes far beyond personal tragedy into the realm of systemic change, maternal courage, and the invisible leadership that mothers provide every single day. Eden's story begins with every parent's worst nightmare—watching your child suffer and feeling powerless to help. But what makes her story remarkable isn't the injury itself, but what she chose to do about it.

When Crisis Becomes Catalyst

After her child experienced a traumatic vaccine injury, Eden faced a crossroads that many mothers know intimately. She could retreat into silence, accept the situation as unchangeable, and focus solely on her family's private healing. Instead, she chose the harder path—becoming educated, certified as a clinical nutritionist, and diving deep into holistic healing approaches that could help not just her child, but countless others facing similar challenges.

This decision represents something profound about maternal leadership. When our children are threatened, mothers don't just respond emotionally—we become researchers, advocates, and warriors. We develop expertise we never planned to have and courage we never knew we possessed. Eden's transformation from concerned parent to certified professional illustrates how personal pain often becomes the foundation for public purpose.

The founding of the MAHA Mom Coalition wasn't just about creating another organization—it was about recognizing that individual mothers facing these challenges needed community, support, and collective power. Grassroots movements led by mothers have historically driven some of the most significant social changes in our society, from civil rights to environmental protection. Eden understood that her individual advocacy would be amplified exponentially through organized collective action.

The Courage to Challenge Systems

What makes Eden's advocacy particularly powerful is her willingness to challenge systems that many people accept without question. Medical freedom and parental rights aren't abstract political concepts when you're watching your child suffer. They become urgent, personal realities that demand action. Eden's work demonstrates that effective advocacy requires both emotional intelligence and strategic thinking—the ability to channel maternal instincts into systemic change.

Her approach to building the MAHA Mom Coalition reflects sophisticated understanding of how movements grow and create impact. Rather than operating from anger alone, Eden has built a platform that combines personal storytelling with policy advocacy, educational resources with community support. This multifaceted approach recognizes that lasting change requires both hearts and minds, both individual transformation and structural reform.

The coalition's focus on medical freedom speaks to broader questions about autonomy, choice, and the relationship between families and institutions. These aren't simple issues with easy answers, but they're conversations that require the voices of mothers who have lived these experiences firsthand. Eden's leadership provides a model for how personal experience can inform public policy without sacrificing nuance or compassion.

Global Impact Through Local Leadership

Perhaps even more remarkable than Eden's domestic advocacy is her decade-long commitment to combating child trafficking on a global scale. Her work has taken her to places like Cameroon and Guatemala, where she has witnessed firsthand the devastating impact of human trafficking, sex trafficking, and organ trafficking on children and families. This global perspective enriches her local advocacy and demonstrates how maternal leadership can extend far beyond our immediate communities.

The connection between Eden's domestic and international work isn't coincidental—it reflects a comprehensive understanding that children's welfare is threatened by different forces in different contexts, but the underlying principle remains the same. Whether fighting for medical freedom in America or working against trafficking in Guatemala, Eden's advocacy is rooted in the fundamental belief that children deserve protection and parents deserve the right to make informed decisions about their families' welfare.

This global perspective also highlights how motherhood creates a unique form of leadership that transcends cultural and national boundaries. The protective instincts that drive Eden's work in her own community are the same instincts that compel her to advocate for vulnerable children thousands of miles away. This expansion of maternal concern from our own children to all children represents leadership at its most fundamental level.

The Power of Storytelling in Leadership

Throughout her advocacy work, Eden has recognized that storytelling isn't just a communication tool—it's a leadership strategy. Personal narratives have the power to make abstract policy issues concrete and urgent. When Eden shares her family's story, she's not just providing information; she's creating connection, empathy, and understanding that can motivate others to action.

This understanding of storytelling as leadership reflects broader truths about how change happens in our society. Data and statistics inform, but stories inspire. Policy papers educate, but personal narratives motivate. Eden's ability to share her family's experience in ways that respect their privacy while advancing the larger cause demonstrates sophisticated leadership skills that many professional advocates struggle to develop.

The ripple effects of Eden's storytelling extend far beyond her immediate audience. Every mother who hears her story and recognizes her own situation, every parent who feels less alone in their advocacy, every community member who becomes more aware of these issues—these represent the multiplying impact of courageous leadership rooted in authentic experience.

Eden Marie James's journey from injury to advocacy illustrates something essential about maternal leadership: it's not about having all the answers or following a predetermined path. It's about refusing to let your children's suffering be in vain, about transforming personal pain into purposeful action, and about recognizing that your individual story has the power to create collective change. Her work reminds us that some of the most important leadership in our world happens not in boardrooms or political offices, but in the hearts and minds of mothers who decide that silence is not an option.

📚 Grab your copy of The Manage Her

quiz.title: What's Your Advocacy Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally create change in the world. Every woman has the power to lead and advocate — but we all do it differently.

questions.question: When you see an injustice or problem in your community, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Jump in immediately to protect those being harmed
Start conversations to understand different viewpoints
Research thoroughly before taking any action
Map out the systemic issues and potential solutions

questions.question: Your child comes home upset about something unfair happening at school. How do you handle it?

options.text: Call the school immediately to address the situation
Talk to other parents to see if they've noticed similar issues
Listen carefully and help your child process their feelings first
Gather information about school policies and procedures

questions.question: What motivates you most when taking on a leadership role?

options.text: Protecting people who can't protect themselves
Bringing people together around a common cause
Being a steady, reliable presence others can count on
Creating lasting systems that prevent future problems

questions.question: When facing a major personal challenge, you typically:

options.text: Channel your emotions into action and advocacy
Reach out to your network for support and advice
Take time to learn and understand before making decisions
Analyze the situation from multiple angles to find solutions

questions.question: Your ideal way to create change in the world would be:

options.text: Leading a passionate movement that fights for justice
Building coalitions that unite diverse groups of people
Mentoring individuals one-on-one to reach their potential
Developing comprehensive policies or programs

questions.question: When telling your story or sharing your experiences, you focus most on:

options.text: The emotional journey and why it matters so deeply
How it brought people together and created community
The lessons learned and wisdom gained along the way
The insights it provides about larger patterns and systems

results.title: The Fierce Protector
results.description: You lead with your heart and fierce determination when something matters to you. Like Eden Marie James, you have the courage to transform personal pain into powerful purpose. You're not afraid to challenge systems that aren't working and fight for what's right, especially when it involves protecting others. This episode will inspire you to see how your protective instincts are actually leadership superpowers that can create lasting change.

results.title: The Bridge Builder
results.description: You create change through connection and collaboration. You have a natural gift for bringing different perspectives together and finding common ground where others see division. Your leadership style focuses on building relationships and creating sustainable solutions through community. Eden's story of building grassroots movements will resonate with your belief that real change happens when we work together and lift each other up.

results.title: The Quiet Revolutionary
results.description: You prefer to lead by example and create change through consistent, thoughtful action. You may not be the loudest voice in the room, but your impact runs deep through the lives you touch personally. You believe in the power of education, preparation, and showing up authentically. This episode will encourage you to see how your behind-the-scenes influence and steady commitment to your values are essential forms of leadership.

results.title: The Strategic Catalyst
results.description: You see the big picture and naturally think about systems, strategies, and long-term impact. Your leadership style involves analyzing situations thoroughly and finding the most effective ways to create meaningful change. You're drawn to understanding root causes and developing comprehensive solutions. Eden's journey from personal crisis to policy advocacy will inspire you to see how strategic thinking combined with passionate purpose can drive real transformation.

transcript: [music] Hi everyone and welcome back to the manager, the podcast where we shine a light on the invisible labor of women and reframe it as leadership. I'm your host Amy Rickabus, the author of The Manager, entrepreneur, and mama of six. Today's guest is a true mama bear, Eden Marie James. Her story begins with a moment no mother should face, a traumatic vaccine injury that changed her son's life and hers forever. But Eden didn't just survive that experience. She became an advocate and a fighter, not only for her children, but for all of ours. She's the founder of the Maha Moms Coalition, a grassroots movement that launched in 2025, standing at the intersection of medical freedom, maternal leadership, and fierce protection of children's rights. But Eden's work doesn't stop there. From 2011 to 2019, she served in global nonprofit efforts advocating for children affected by sex trafficking, human trafficking, and organ trafficking. She's traveled the world, raised awareness, hosted fundraisers, and spoken out against systemic failures, all while raising her kids and using her voice as a powerful tool for change. In this episode, it's about turning pain into purpose, about using your voice when no one else will, and about one person can do to spark a movement. Let's dive in. Hey Eden, thank you so much for being here. >> I need you to follow me around and give that intro everywhere I go. [laughter] I'm just saying like I mean like wow. Thank you so much. I'm so [laughter] happy to be here. >> I'm so glad. I'm so happy to have you. I'm really honored to have you on the show. So, let's start at the beginning. Can you tell us about what happened to your son at the age of 1 and how did that moment shift everything for you? >> So my oldest child is 30 years old. So this is 29 years ago. So you have to think um you know this is in 9495 there was no internet there were technology was not like it was today. Um moms were you know reading books and that's how we found our form of education right we didn't we didn't have the resources we have today. But I gave birth to my first child. I had a lingering um just like a objection, an apprehension, uh mother's intuition that did not something didn't feel right to me about vaccinating him. Um I had no reason to feel that way logically. I hadn't really I mean, like I said, there was no internet. I was reading some things. I I hadn't encountered anything that really made me think um no, don't do this. But I had this terrible gut feeling. So when he was born, I held off for the first six months. I said, "No, no, no. I'm going to wait." And I was trying to kind of voraciously like tear through material and find what I could, which there were very few resources at that time. You I slowly started the process of inoculating him. I had a pediatrician that was an older doctor and he had this darling little Japanese nurse who was also older who they were both very stern and very strict with me. And I was 20 when I had my baby. And at this point, I'm 21, but I'm a young, naive mom. Um, I was living in Hawaii at the time. My family wasn't near me. And they really pressured me to vaccinate him. They She went so far, the nurse went so far as to tell me that I was being a terrible mother. I was harming my child by making the choices that I was making, that I was being irresponsible, that I was young and stupid, and I just didn't know. And in her defense, you know, um, she was an older woman. I don't know what she lived through. I don't know what she saw on her journey and what you know moved her into the space where she had formulated that belief system but she felt very strongly about it and ultimately I did succumb to that. He got through the first round and he changed overnight. He was a really happy baby. He started crying all the time. I took him back to the pediatrician and I was like, "Hey, like what's going on with my child?" And they were like, "He has collic." I'm like, "He's six months old." Like that's when I first started. He was six months. I'm like, "You don't get collic at six months. Like babies are born with that." And if and that's a whole another conversation in and of itself, right? But flash forward, I held off again. I held off. I didn't do any nine month shots, 10 month shots. And it was a couple months after his first birthday, I agreed to allow him to receive his MMR vaccination >> and um he got it. It's this is a really emotional subject for me, so I'm going to try to write, [sighs] you know, like keep it together. I'm with you. >> He got it and he had like an aniflactic shock, allergic reaction to it. His airway closed up. Um he was unable to breathe. I had to rush him to the emergency room. We were then subsequently in the emergency room between urgent care and the emergency room seven times over the next week. He was then diagnosed overnight with being like full-blown asthmatic. I had him um on breathing treatments multiple times per day. We couldn't leave the house without an inhaler in addition to what was happening in the medical community with his treatment. And at that time, you know, again, this is 29 years ago, I asked the medical team like what happened and they said he had an allergic reaction to his vaccination. You cannot vaccinate him like no moving forward, >> right? >> Um and it wasn't so taboo, right, what was communicated to me. >> Yeah. >> They um were very forthright with it. There was no shame in it. They said, "Some kids react this way. We don't know who which ones are going to. We're terribly sorry you're going through this." >> Um but that would be our advice for you moving forward. But at that time, um, it's still from a mother's perspective, like not listening. It's something I carry a tremendous amount of guilt and shame around because I feel like I had the wherewithal and the intuition as a mom not to do it. Like everything inside of me was screaming no and I was listening to a lot of outside forces and a lot of outside chatter and voices. >> Um, and then he I mean, he's okay now, right? But um we had to I that's really when my journey with alternative health and wellness began because I started then looking into alternative ways to deal with his now chronic health conditions. Right. >> Right. He became very susceptible to all illness. We were dealing with his breathing issues. Um his sleep patterns his he developed food allergies overnight. I mean like everything got turned upside down. His sleep I mean like that was another thing like the child stopped sleeping like I mean it was really really a challenging time. So I started looking for alternative resources. Um and it's really when my education as well like I enrolled in a school in Hawaii called the Holistic University of the Pacific. I started studying alternative modalities, healing, nutrition um and it really I mean it's I would have preferred that my child hadn't gone through that right as a mother but subsequently as a result of it it really changed the trajectory of my life and his life and then the lives of so many others that um my advocacy has impacted and me just having the information that that I have as a result of personal learned experience. So that really was what catapulted me onto that journey and I never really looked back. >> Amazing. >> Yeah. It also really taught me too that like you have to ask questions. It's okay to ask questions. It's if you have apprehension about something, you need to lean into that and a and soo yourself, get the answers that you need and that you seek in order to find comfort with the decision that you're making. Right. >> Absolutely. >> And so it strengthened my ability also to use my voice. Yes. Right. And it also supported me in really learning how to lean into my own intuition and when to say yes to my my inner dialogue, right, to that higher self that was actually guiding me. So that's amazing. It's amazing the the stories that I hear from women often, you know, their experiences with their first child often changed their trajectory of their lives in usually in the betterment of humanity as a whole. Well, I mean, they also it having a child just blew my heart wide open like in a whole another way. Like the feeling of love that moves through you on every single level. You were like, "Oh my gosh, I didn't even know this was possible." >> It's amazing. >> But it, you know, I looking back on that time, it was a very difficult time, right? It was, >> you know, you're a mom of six, right? You know, [laughter] like, and I was I was only had one and I was young, right? But um I was also putting myself through school and I was you know trying to I was navigating a world very much on my own. >> Yeah. You know. >> Yeah. And you were young. >> Yeah. I was I was really young. >> Yeah. >> I'm so proud of you what you were able to do at such a young age to make that pivot and to to have that intuition to know that you needed to get into that alternative healing. >> I'm the oldest of five children. >> So I I feel like I was also my the foundation of my life also set me up for that. Um, I was mothering littles. My mom had four kids in six years. >> Wow. >> So, there were a lot of little babies around all the time and I was very maternal in that way. And so, I feel like I also was kind of wired and um that the preemptive foundation had supported me also in finding that path. >> Absolutely. I know it's God has a plan for all of us. I feel like there's just [clears throat] this divine plan that we, you know, you don't see it when you're in it, but as you get older, you start to see like, oh, okay. Okay, I kind of see that makes sense. Oh, no. I see where that happened. Like, oh, okay. That was supporting me also in, you know, we also don't realize too sometimes something that's going to happen maybe 5, 10, 15, 20 years in the past that is then subsequently going to support us in a future endeavor. >> Absolutely. >> You know, it's like I actually started doing some um clinical nutrition training with Pam Popper. >> Yeah. >> Which, you know, this is >> 25 years ago, right? And um I think maybe a year, no it was maybe a couple years ago, maybe it was like on the tail end of all the COVID stuff, I started seeing her pop up all over social media talking about things and I was like, "Wait, how is that name familiar? I know that woman." And then I was like, "Wow, what a full circle moment, right?" And I also was really proud. I was like, "Oh, I I trained with her 25 years ago, and here she still is out on the front lines, you [laughter] know, talking about the importance of, you know, good food choices and nutrition and um, you know, the health and vitality of our fruits and vegetables, which sounds crazy to use health and vitality with food, but essentially it is a, you know, a very integral component of, you know, all the things that we're consuming, right?" >> Yes. So anyway, yeah, I mean it was all those things kind of came back around and you know, I realize now that God definitely had a plan and that so many things were being laid down for me that would then support me in future iterations of myself. >> Yeah. And you didn't just advocate for your own child, you became a part of a much wider movement. And what does medical freedom mean for you personally? >> Well, it's interesting. Um, you know, I I want to say something, you know, really quickly. It was really interesting going through that experience with my son like I was not an antivaxer, >> right? I had some apprehension about making those decisions, >> but I was not an antivaxer. And also part of what you know you're saying, what what's my definition of medical freedom? Right. My my my definition of medical freedom is actually being able to make informed decisions and choices, right? Being able to >> walk into um your pediatrician's office and say, "I'd like to see the inserts." >> Sure. >> Right. I'd like to have a deeper conversation about this. I'd like to have access to more resources. And then being able to find out the answers you're seeking and make your informed decision and have that decision be respected, >> right, >> by the people that are around you. It also is not one-sizefits-all medicine, right? Um >> yeah. Not even not everyone in the US could take a Tylenol >> or any other number of substances that are out there, right? And if I were to say like, "Oh, I can't take that because I have a reaction to it." That doesn't make me an anti- Tylenol. Tylenol. >> Exactly. If you if you are allergic to penicellin, you're not anti penicellin. >> No, I just I'm allergic to penicellin. [laughter] Take it way. >> We don't have anything against penicellin in general. It may be great for other people. [laughter] >> Yeah. I mean it's how how this whole um >> this stigmatism that has been created around this like you know you have so many moms who are walking around with extremely injured children >> right you know I feel very fortunate my son is high functioning right >> and I was able at that time to make the best decisions once I you know once I had this experience and I was able to find the ability to stand strong in my truth and say no more I'm not doing this anymore. more that there are so many parents, especially in the state that we're in, that they don't have that option. >> Unfortunately, no. The law in California is if your child has an anaphylactic reaction to one vaccine, your child must receive the other vaccines to prove that they're allergic to the other vaccines. >> I'm just like, I can't. >> That is the law in California. Yeah. No, there is no medical exemption for you if you have anaphylactic shock. >> Yeah. I have a friend who had a child who had a reaction like that and she was advised by her pediatrician, well the next time that you do it, just do it at the hospital so that the medical professionals there are ready to attend to your child if your child is potentially on the brink of dying and succumbing to death from the anaphylactic shock reaction. And I was like I how did someone say that to you with a straight face? I just I'm not sure how you can call it medicine when you know first do no harm >> is the first rule. >> I also think that like Hawaii is the perfect example. So Hawaii right now um there is legislature I don't know if it was actually signed into law. It was being voted on and they were talking about it in the last couple months where they were going to start removing exemptions. Oh yes. Right. >> And this is a state where there haven't been any outbreaks or epidemics of anything. Right. Everyone's been doing just fine, right? And this small subsect of the population of these children that need exemptions because they have a medical condition or even because they have a personal belief exemption, right? You know, they don't it's a religious exemption. Whatever it is, it's a very small population of children. >> And there's been no issues at all whatsoever. There's been no issues whatsoever in relation to any of this, right? All the kids that are going to school, everyone's fine. Um, but for some reason they've decided to target this very tiny population of families and children that need these exemptions. And it really makes no logical sense like why why are they doing that? >> I honestly and even in the state of California, I don't understand what the purpose was of them coming after the children with with the medical exemptions. I really did not understand that. And I feel like when they did that, when they do that, when they come after this vulnerable population of children, >> they wake up a big old monster they didn't mean to wake up. And that's the mama bear monster. >> Yeah. I was at the capital when that bill was moving its way through. And I have never seen anything like that in my life. I mean, >> me neither. I was there. >> Yeah. I mean, you had thousands of mothers, hundreds of them banging on the walls, right? um crying, shouting, you know, begging, pleading, right? You had injured children everywhere like it >> in wheelchairs and with paralytic legs in splints on so many things like I mean it it just being there and being in that moment and being in that energy and witnessing that and seeing that um and they definitely awoke a spirit um deep within so many mothers and I feel like when other mothers across the nation saw that >> Mhm. and saw what they were witness what these moms were going through and they they bore witness to that that it awoke a cry within. I mean, you know, I hear I heard something last week someone said to me and I I don't I don't know if it was on a news station or where it was, but it was coming through mainstream media where they were saying like the political establishment is more concerned and afraid of the Maha movement, right, than any other political movement that has occurred, right? because they have awoken and stirred something within hundreds of thousands and millions of mothers, right? And like you stand between a mother and their child >> like and I will stab you with a fork. [laughter] >> Whatever I can find, buddy. >> I hate to say that, right? But I mean like like every single it's like fire comes out of my eyes and my ears and I am awakened in a way that is it is um it's unexplainable. >> It's primal. >> It's Yes. It's primal and I really don't think they knew that I I don't think they knew it was going to happen because now we have these women who've been in this movement like you you've been in it for 29 years. I joined it 10 years ago when it started affecting my children as soon as they were taking away their religious and philosophical exemptions. I just my children um have a methylation defect called MTHFR. I have a 1.5 times mthr not defect um mutated >> mutated yeah mutations gene mutations for methylation and it's um you know when you know that your children are more prone to being injured than other people's children. It's not something you want to do to them and it's something that your doctor understood this is a legitimate reason for you to not do this practice. Well, doctors also, medical doctors also understand that, you know, there are statistics about this. There are a number of children that they know are going to have an adverse reaction, right? And they might say it's one in a 100 or 10 in 100, but they really expect that you put your child in that line of 100 children, and you just wait to see whichever one, >> you know, is the one that has the dart thrown at them that's going to have that allergic reaction or is going to have that >> autoimmune is what I'm worried about cuz my mother has juvenile rheumatoid arthritis and ankalyzing spondylosis. her spine is basically degenerating in her body. And it's to see that kind of degeneration that she and the pain that she's dealt with her whole life from these autoimmune diseases. For me, it's just not worth it to protect my children from something that they could be sick with for a week or two weeks. >> God forbid, right? Let's hypothetically say that one of them contracted measles, right? Like you keep your child at home. Well, I mean, there's always that running to that joke. It's not a joke, but you see that parody where they're showing on um the Brady Bunch what it used to be like when a child had the measles. Like, oh man, I got to stay home from school for a week. I got the measles, right? And the whole family is talking about it like I had chickenpox, right? And I sat home for a week and I watched MTV. It was like a really big exciting time in my life cuz I got to I was a little itchy, right? You know, but otherwise like >> my whole my mom actually was like, "Oh, all your brothers and sisters, everybody go and get the chickenpox." >> You do it and it was one and done. Everybody in the house got it. >> Exactly. >> My brother and I had it at the same time. So, we had a company. We got to hang out together. >> We did the same thing. We all hung out and it was like, "Okay, this is it." >> It was a right of passage. >> Well, I mean, I don't want to say that it was an exciting and enjoyable thing to have the chickenpox. there was something that actually was um it was very tribal in my family like we all got together and we all were there and we all had this thing and we hung out um and it's not to say that I don't recognize that there are children that deal with um you know viruses differently >> absolutely and some kids are you know their immune systems are impaired and we understand that but so >> and also circling back there are children whose immune systems are impaired which is why they cannot receive an inoculation and why they like your children shouldn't be subjected to the normal um vaccine schedule the way that we see it today. >> And we had a doctor who very much understood that and we had an exemption that that you know for that very reason and when the law changed that was no longer an acceptable reason uh not to be vaccinated in the state of California. So my children have been denied the right to an education since 2020. >> Yeah. When my daughter was born, um, I held off for many, many years. I demanded that an immunologist would be the one that if I were to make that decision and I were to inoculate her that I would be working with an immunologist. I asked that shots be broken up, right? And [clears throat] that nothing was done as a cocktail, right? And that um, you know, and this is all possible. Like these things are all possible. They're just not the easy path. >> And it's hard, especially I mean, there are some really hard ones. Like I think MMR comes together as one. I don't think you can just I don't think that you can choose one or the like just >> this was 23 years ago when she was born and so at that time they were make there was an option to do that. >> Um but at the same time you know the pediatric practice that we went to >> um there were all medical doctors there and one nurse practitioner and they all knew very clearly what my stance was and what my experience was. because they had met my son as well. And all of the medical doctors even back then refused to see me. Only the nurse practitioner would see me. >> Wow. Even all the all the way back then cuz now you always get fired. I mean I I have so many friends who have had, you know, had children that with the same condition as mine whose pediatricians would know would kick them out of the practice. >> Yeah. Cuz they want that big pharma kickback from that shot. >> Well, most people don't realize that pediatricians get a paycheck at the end of the year if they have a 100% vaccination rate within their clinic. and the paycheck is about $250,000 a year. >> Yeah. It's a really substantial amount of money and they lose that if they don't have whole practice participation, right? >> So that's really why, you know, you take it personally. You think it's about you as you know, you're a terrible person, but you comes down to money in the end. And it's so sad that they've made mothers feel like bad people, but it was really never about them feeling that way.
